fields.label: Grant Amount

fields.help: Total vesting period in years (typically 4 years for tech startups).

fields.help: Expected annual stock price appreciation as a percentage. Use 8-12% as typical tech growth, or adjust based on company stage.

example.title: Sample Calculation
steps: Calculate vested value at each milestone

explanation:
**Stock options and equity compensation** are standard in tech careers. Understanding vesting schedules helps you evaluate job offers and plan your financial future.

## How Stock Vesting Works

Most tech companies use a 4-year vesting schedule with a 1-year cliff:
- **Cliff (Year 1)**: 25% of shares vest
- **Years 2–4**: Remaining 75% vests monthly (about 2.08% per month)
- **If you leave early**: Unvested shares are forfeited; only vested shares are yours

## Standard Vesting Schedule Breakdown

| Time | Vested % | Cumulative | Stock Price Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 year (cliff) | 25% | 25% | Early valuation |
| 2 years | 25% | 50% | Mid-stage growth |
| 3 years | 25% | 75% | Scaling growth |
| 4 years | 25% | 100% | Mature valuation |

## Key Equity Principles

- **Understand the cliff**: Leaving before the 1-year cliff means losing 25% of your grant
- **Stock price matters**: Your equity value depends entirely on company valuation at exercise
- **Exercise window**: After leaving, you typically have 90 days to buy shares before options expire
- **Tax implications**: ISOs (Incentive) vs NSOs (Non-Qualified) have different tax treatments
- **Don't concentrate**: Company stock shouldn't dominate your net worth—diversify
- **Long-term play**: Equity is compensation for staying and contributing over years

## Final Notes

This calculator is an estimation tool. For critical decisions involving equity, taxes, or employment terms, consult a tax professional or employment attorney. Values are current for 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.q: How does stock vesting actually work?
faq.a: Most tech companies grant you shares over a 4-year period. You don't own them immediately. Typically 25% vest after 1 year (cliff), then the remaining 75% vests monthly over the next 3 years. Only vested shares are yours.

faq.q: What happens to my unvested stock if I leave the company?
faq.a: Unvested shares are forfeited—returned to the company. You only keep what's already vested. This is why the cliff period matters so much: leaving at 11 months costs you 25% of your grant.

faq.q: What's the difference between ISOs and NSOs?
faq.a: ISOs (Incentive Stock Options) get tax-favorable treatment if you hold long enough. NSOs (Non-Qualified) are taxed as ordinary income at exercise. ISOs have stricter eligibility rules and limits.

faq.q: How long do I have to exercise options after leaving my job?
faq.a: Most companies give 90 days after departure to exercise vested options. This is a hard deadline—missing it forfeits those shares. Plan ahead if you're considering leaving.
Yes. During acquisitions or change of control, companies often accelerate vesting. Some have single-trigger (automatic) or double-trigger (requires job loss). Negotiate this in your offer letter.

Exercise of options is a taxable event. The gain (stock price minus strike price) is taxed—ordinary income for NSOs, potentially capital gains for ISOs. Get advice from a CPA who handles stock compensation.

faq.q: What's the strike price and why does it matter?
faq.a: Strike price is what you pay to exercise options (typically FMV when granted). Your profit = (current stock price − strike price) × shares. Lower strike = more profit at exit.
We use standard vesting formulas. Results are estimates—stock price appreciation is speculative. For major decisions, consult a financial advisor or tax professional.
Yes. All Hacé Cuentas calculators are completely free with no registration, no ads, and no data collection.
